item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, this is giving me chills. I had a playlist in the background as I read it, and I nearly jumped when I heard something vaguely like laughing. The imagery of the face mask with laughing gas was, ugh. I was going to pass this off as spooky without meaning behind it at first, but reading the comments made me take a second look.

I'm thinking that the subjects become something like actors in a sitcom, hearing laugh tracks from the left or right (presumably where the live audience would be). Talico had a magic broadcaster that would add a laugh track (that would infect humans who heard it long enough) if a show didn't already have one, is my take.

I like how this explains why comedies all have those darn laugh tracks nowadays.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hah, no it's not meant to be. The name similarity hadn't even occurred to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is that a lowkey reference to Tommy Tallarico?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah that's actually intentional - I had considered adding a note specifically pointing out that they didn't know why he wasn't found when he was originally reported missing, but I decided against it. In retrospect that might have been a mistake, but never mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The final addendum was very nice but I found it strange that the police didn't check the basement when he was reported missing. I also think it would have been creepier if the laughter wasn't described as manic but was just ordinary canned laughter that just occurred in the wrong place and too loudly.

Anyway, whilst it covers topics that have been explored before, the explanation at the end really brought this to firm upvote territory +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only trope I can think of that he might be refering to is "thing that affects TV programing", but this SCP is definitely original enough that it doesn't matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was very difficult to resist the urge to just flat out describe it as Flanderization, heh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These changes are typically expressed as the extreme exaggeration of one or more aspects of the individuals personality or demeanour to the point that they override all other observable traits.

"In the eyes of God, they'll be Flanderseseses!"

Flanderization ruins lives. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! And, I definitely wanted the device to remain a mystery, having testing logs for it would have taken away from that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The 2nd addendum really was the clincher for me- reading through it at first, I was expecting not to have any sort of explanation behind it, and for it to stay in the realm of somewhat of a cliche, although identifying what cliche it is is difficult… I don't know. I liked the half-reason behind the phenomenon, that really gives it a sense of mystery and reminds me of an X-files episode.

I think an interesting addition could be experiment logs with the broadcasting device, although that could take away from the vibe you've cultivated. Also, classifying those affected as SCP-3566-1,2,3… might be a good edit.

Nice work! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Glad you liked it. Not sure I know which part you mean is a common trope, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well that took a turn near the end.

Going for a common trope like this can be risky, but I think you pulled it off well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a great interpretation and pretty close to what I was thinking (though I won't say exactly what that was, as you said I want there to be some small mystery about it).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is creepy-good.

Things like this, that give anomalous context to mundane real-life phenomena, are some of my favorites to read, and it's used to great effect here.

Well-written throughout, and I enjoyed the last addendum's conservation of mystery. I can see what the guy did, but I can't really tell what he did, or what's actually happening to him, which lets my imagination wander. Some puzzle pieces are there, but putting them together is challenging.

My personal interpretation is that he tried to put himself into a television show somehow with an anomalous tool, but it didn't quite work, and instead of passing through the fourth wall, his soul stopped inside it, and only a small part of 'him', his raw sense of humor, was able to pull all the way through and react to the funny-funny of the stuff in TV-land. This was fine with him at first. If this was as close as he could get to being a part of a television show, so be it. It was funny, and he wanted to be closer to that funny. How was he to know that a raw personality trait, when exposed to others without being naturally buffered by other personality traits, was hazardous to mental health?

Then one day, there was some kind of accident with his device. His soul kind of snapped off, and his body died completely, while his soul became irreversibly stuck inside the fourth wall. Then it stopped being funny.

That's probably all wrong. xD But it's fun to think and muse about stuff like this.

Good job! Hearty +1!

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to the following for reading the draft of this:

CaptainKirby
MaliceAforethought
NineVolt
Weryillium
taylor_itkin
drblackbox

Read more of my stuff here!
